sections.heading: This Prudential Standard is made under:
sections.title: Prudential Standard authority basis
sections.summary: This section specifies the legislative basis for the Prudential Standard, stating it is enacted under five specific sections of Australian financial legislation: Banking Act 1959, Insurance Act 1973, Life Insurance Act 1995, Private Health Insurance (Prudential Supervision) Act 2015, and Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993. The section establishes the legal foundation for the Prudential Standard's authority.

requirements.text: This Prudential Standard is made under section 11AF of the Banking Act 1959
This Prudential Standard is made under section 32 of the Insurance Act 1973
This Prudential Standard is made under section 230A of the Life Insurance Act 1995
This Prudential Standard is made under section 92 of the Private Health Insurance (Prudential Supervision) Act 2015
This Prudential Standard is made under section 34C of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993

sections.source_quote: This Prudential Standard is made under: (a) section 11AF of the Banking Act 1959 (Banking Act); (b) section 32 of the Insurance Act 1973 (Insurance Act); (c) section 230A of the Life Insurance Act 1995 (Life Insurance Act); (d) section 92 of the Private Health Insurance (Prudential Supervision) Act 2015 (PHIPS Act); and (e) section 34C of the Superannuation Industry (Supervision) Act 1993 (SIS Act).
sections.relevance_reason: irrelevant: governance, not data compliance

sections.heading: This Prudential Standard applies to all ‘APRA-regulated entities' defined as:
sections.title: APRA-regulated entities covered by the standard
sections.summary: This section defines which entities fall under the Prudential Standard by specifying APRA-regulated entities, including authorized deposit-taking institutions, general insurers, life companies, private health insurers, and superannuation industry licensees. The scope covers both Australian and foreign entities operating within these categories.

requirements.text: This Prudential Standard applies to all 'APRA-regulated entities' defined as: (a) authorized deposit-taking institutions (ADIs), including foreign ADIs, and non-operating holding companies authorized under the Banking Act

This Prudential Standard applies to all 'APRA-regulated entities' defined as: (b) general insurers, including Category C insurers, non-operating holding companies authorized under the Insurance Act, and parent entities of Level 2 insurance groups

This Prudential Standard applies to all 'APRA-regulated entities' defined as: (c) life companies, including friendly societies, eligible foreign life insurance companies (EFLICs) and non-operating holding companies registered under the Life Insurance Act
This Prudential Standard applies to all 'APRA-regulated entities' defined as: (d) private health insurers registered under the PHIPS Act
This Prudential Standard applies to all 'APRA-regulated entities' defined as: (e) RSE licensees under the SIS Act in respect of their business operations

sections.source_quote: This Prudential Standard applies to all 'APRA-regulated entities' defined as: (a) authorized deposit-taking institutions (ADIs), including foreign ADIs, and non-operating holding companies authorized under the Banking Act (authorized banking NOHCs); (b) general insurers, including Category C insurers, non-operating holding companies authorized under the Insurance Act (authorised insurance NOHCs), and parent entities of Level 2 insurance groups; (c) life companies, including friendly societies, eligible foreign life insurance companies (EFLICs) and non-operating holding companies registered under the Life Insurance Act (registered life NOHCs); (d) private health insurers registered under the PHIPS Act; and (e) RSE licensees under the SIS Act in respect of their business operations.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines entities subject to data compliance

sections.heading: The obligations imposed by this Prudential Standard on, or in relation to, a foreign
sections.title: Foreign entities' Australian branch operations scope
sections.summary: This section limits the application of the Prudential Standard to Australian branch operations of foreign entities. It specifies that obligations only apply to foreign ADIs, Category C insurers, or EFLICs in relation to their Australian branch activities, excluding other operations.

requirements.text: The obligations imposed by this Prudential Standard on, or in relation to, a foreign ADI, a Category C insurer or an EFLIC apply only in relation to the Australian branch operations of that entity

sections.source_quote: The obligations imposed by this Prudential Standard on, or in relation to, a foreign ADI, a Category C insurer or an EFLIC apply only in relation to the Australian branch operations of that entity.
sections.relevance_reason: Applies to foreign entities' branch operations scope for data compliance

sections.heading: Where an APRA-regulated entity is the ‘Head of a group',2 it must comply with
sections.title: Group compliance requirements
sections.summary: This section mandates that group entities must comply with the Prudential Standard in their capacity as APRA-regulated entities, ensuring requirements are applied appropriately throughout the group, including non-regulated entities, and on a group basis. It clarifies that references to 'APRA-regulated entity' in the standard apply to 'Head of a group' and 'entity' to 'group' when applying requirements group-wide.

requirements.text: Where an APRA-regulated entity is the 'Head of a group', it must comply with a requirement of this Prudential Standard in its capacity as an APRA-regulated entity

Where an APRA-regulated entity is the 'Head of a group', it must ensure that the requirement is applied appropriately throughout the group, including in relation to entities that are not APRA-regulated
Where an APRA-regulated entity is the 'Head of a group', it must comply with the requirement on a group basis
In applying the requirements of this Prudential Standard on a group basis, references in paragraphs 13 to 36 to an 'APRA-regulated entity' are to be read as 'Head of a group'
In applying the requirements of this Prudential Standard on a group basis, references to 'entity' are to be read as 'group'

sections.source_quote: Where an APRA-regulated entity is the 'Head of a group', it must comply with a requirement of this Prudential Standard: (a) in its capacity as an APRA-regulated entity; (b) by ensuring that the requirement is applied appropriately throughout the group, including in relation to entities that are not APRA-regulated; and (c) on a group basis.
sections.relevance_reason: Group compliance for APRA-regulated entities

sections.heading: Where an APRA-regulated entity's information assets are managed by a third
sections.title: Third-party information asset compliance timeline
sections.summary: This section establishes when the Prudential Standard applies to information assets managed by third parties, specifying that requirements take effect from the earlier of the next renewal date of the third-party contract or 1 July 2020. This ensures timely compliance with data management obligations.

requirements.text: Where an APRA-regulated entity's information assets are managed by a third party, the requirements in this Prudential Standard will apply in relation to those information assets from the earlier of the next renewal date of the contract with the third party or 1 July 2020

sections.source_quote: Where an APRA-regulated entity's information assets are managed by a third party, the requirements in this Prudential Standard will apply in relation to those information assets from the earlier of the next renewal date of the contract with the third party or 1 July 2020.
sections.relevance_reason: Third-party compliance timeline for data management

sections.heading: Terms that are defined in Prudential Standard APS 001 Definitions (APS 001),
sections.title: Definition usage in standard
sections.summary: This section states that terms defined in other Prudential Standards (APS 001, GPS 001, LPS 001, HPS 001, 3PS 001) appear in bold for the first time they are used in this Prudential Standard. This ensures consistency in terminology across regulatory documents.

requirements.text: Terms that are defined in Prudential Standard APS 001 Definitions (APS 001), Prudential Standard GPS 001 Definitions (GPS 001), Prudential Standard LPS 001 Definitions, Prudential Standard HPS 001 Definitions or Prudential Standard 3PS 001 Definitions appear in bold the first time they are used in this Prudential Standard

sections.source_quote: Terms that are defined in Prudential Standard APS 001 Definitions (APS 001), Prudential Standard GPS 001 Definitions (GPS 001), Prudential Standard LPS 001 Definitions, Prudential Standard HPS 001 Definitions or Prudential Standard 3PS 001 Definitions appear in bold the first time they are used in this Prudential Standard.
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance terminology consistency

sections.heading: In this Prudential Standard, unless the contrary intention appears, a reference to
sections.title: Reference to Acts and Standards
sections.summary: This section clarifies that references to Acts, Regulations, or Prudential Standards within the standard refer to the current in-force versions. It establishes the temporal scope for such references without specifying any particular version or amendment.

requirements.text: References to Acts, Regulations, or Prudential Standards refer to the current in-force versions

sections.source_quote: In this Prudential Standard, unless the contrary intention appears, a reference to an Act, Regulation or Prudential Standard is a reference to the Act, Regulation or Prudential Standard as in force from time to time.
sections.relevance_reason: commencement/assent dates

sections.heading: For the purposes of this Prudential Standard:
sections.title: Group and Head Definitions
sections.summary: This section defines key terms such as 'group', 'Head of a group', 'Level 2 group', and 'Level 2 Head' to clarify the organizational structure and leadership roles within APRA-regulated entities. These definitions are critical for understanding the scope of regulatory oversight and responsibilities.

requirements.text: ‘group' means a Level 2 group or a Level 3 group, as relevant
‘Head of a group' means a Level 2 Head or Level 3 Head, as relevant
‘Level 2 group' means the entities that comprise: (a) Level 2 as defined in APS 001; or (b) a Level 2 insurance group as defined in GPS 001

‘Level 2 Head' means: (a) where an ADI that is a member of a Level 2 group is not a subsidiary of an authorised banking NOHC or another ADI, that ADI; (b) where an ADI that is a member of a Level 2 group is a subsidiary of an authorised banking NOHC, that authorised banking NOHC; or (c) the parent entity of a Level 2 insurance group as defined in GPS 001

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of this Prudential Standard: ‘group' means a Level 2 group or a Level 3 group, as relevant; ‘Head of a group' means a Level 2 Head or Level 3 Head, as relevant; ‘Level 2 group' means the entities that comprise: (a) Level 2 as defined in APS 001; or (b) a Level 2 insurance group as defined in GPS 001; ‘Level 2 Head' means: (a) where an ADI that is a member of a Level 2 group is not a subsidiary of an authorised banking NOHC or another ADI, that ADI; (b) where an ADI that is a member of a Level 2 group is a subsidiary of an authorised banking NOHC, that authorised banking NOHC; or (c) the parent entity of a Level 2 insurance group as defined in GPS 001.
sections.relevance_reason: tangential: organizational structure definitions

sections.heading: The following definitions are used in this Prudential Standard:
sections.title: Information Security Definitions
sections.summary: This section provides detailed definitions for information security terms such as availability, confidentiality, criticality, information assets, information security, information security capability, information security controls, information security incidents, information security policy frameworks, information security threats, information security vulnerabilities, integrity, and sensitivity. These definitions are foundational for implementing information security measures.

requirements.text: availability refers to accessibility and usability when required
confidentiality refers to access being restricted only to those authorised
criticality refers to the potential impact of a loss of availability
information asset means information and information technology, including software, hardware and data (both soft and hard copy)
information security means the preservation of an information asset's confidentiality, integrity and availability
information security capability means the totality of resources, skills and controls which provide the ability and capacity to maintain information security
information security control means a prevention, detection or response measure to reduce the likelihood or impact of an information security incident
information security incident means an actual or potential compromise of information security
information security policy framework means the totality of policies, standards, guidelines and procedures pertaining to information security
information security threat (threat) is a circumstance or event that has the potential to exploit an information security vulnerability
information security vulnerability (vulnerability) is a weakness in an information asset or information security control that could be exploited to compromise information security
integrity refers to completeness, accuracy and freedom from unauthorised change or usage
sensitivity means the potential impact of a loss of confidentiality or integrity

sections.source_quote: The following definitions are used in this Prudential Standard: (a) availability refers to accessibility and usability when required; (b) confidentiality refers to access being restricted only to those authorised; (c) criticality refers to the potential impact of a loss of availability; (d) information asset means information and information technology, including software, hardware and data (both soft and hard copy); (e) information security means the preservation of an information asset's confidentiality, integrity and availability; (f) information security capability means the totality of resources, skills and controls which provide the ability and capacity to maintain information security; (g) information security control means a prevention, detection or response measure to reduce the likelihood or impact of an information security incident; (h) information security incident means an actual or potential compromise of information security; (i) information security policy framework means the totality of policies, standards, guidelines and procedures pertaining to information security; (j) information security threat (threat) is a circumstance or event that has the potential to exploit an information security vulnerability; (k) information security vulnerability (vulnerability) is a weakness in an information asset or information security control that could be exploited to compromise information security; (l) integrity refers to completeness, accuracy and freedom from unauthorised change or usage; and (m) sensitivity means the potential impact of a loss of confidentiality or integrity.
sections.relevance_reason: Core definitions for data security and compliance

sections.heading: The Board4 of an APRA-regulated entity (Board) is ultimately responsible for the
sections.title: Board Responsibility for Information Security
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Board of an APRA-regulated entity is ultimately responsible for the information security of the entity. The Board must ensure that information security measures are commensurate with the size and extent of threats to information assets and support the continued sound operation of the entity.

requirements.text: The Board of an APRA-regulated entity is ultimately responsible for the information security of the entity
The Board must ensure that the entity maintains information security in a manner commensurate with the size and extent of threats to its information assets
The Board must ensure that the entity maintains information security which enables the continued sound operation of the entity

sections.source_quote: The Board4 of an APRA-regulated entity (Board) is ultimately responsible for the information security of the entity. The Board must ensure that the entity maintains information security in a manner commensurate with the size and extent of threats to its information assets, and which enables the continued sound operation of the entity.
sections.relevance_reason: Board info security responsibility for APRA entities

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must clearly define the information security-related
sections.title: Definition of Information Security Roles
sections.summary: This section requires APRA-regulated entities to clearly define the information security-related roles and responsibilities of the Board, senior management, governing bodies, and individuals with decision-making authority. This includes oversight, approval, operations, and other information security functions.

requirements.text: An APRA-regulated entity must clearly define the information security-related roles and responsibilities of the Board, senior management, governing bodies and individuals with responsibility for decision-making
An APRA-regulated entity must clearly define the information security-related roles and responsibilities for approval, oversight, operations and other information security functions

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must clearly define the information security-related roles and responsibilities of the Board, senior management, governing bodies and individuals with responsibility for decision-making, approval, oversight, operations and other information security functions.
sections.relevance_reason: APRA governance roles for data security oversight

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must maintain an information security capability
sections.title: Information Security Capability Maintenance
sections.summary: This section requires APRA-regulated entities to maintain an information security capability that is commensurate with the size and extent of threats to their information assets and supports the continued sound operation of the entity. This capability must be sufficient to address evolving risks.

requirements.text: An APRA-regulated entity must maintain an information security capability commensurate with the size and extent of threats to its information assets
An APRA-regulated entity must maintain an information security capability which enables the continued sound operation of the entity

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must maintain an information security capability commensurate with the size and extent of threats to its information assets, and which enables the continued sound operation of the entity.
sections.relevance_reason: Information security capability for APRA entities

sections.heading: Where information assets are managed by a related party or third party, the
sections.title: Assess information security capability of related parties and third parties
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must evaluate the information security capabilities of related parties and third parties managing their information assets. This assessment must consider the potential impact of security incidents on those assets. The requirement applies to all information assets handled by external parties, not just outsourced activities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires assessing third-party security capabilities before transferring data, directly impacting DTIA by mandating risk-based evaluations of external data handlers.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must assess information security capability of related parties and third parties managing information assets
Assessment must be commensurate with potential consequences of security incidents affecting those assets

sections.source_quote: Where information assets are managed by a related party or third party, the APRA-regulated entity must assess the information security capability of that party, commensurate with the potential consequences of an information security incident affecting those assets.
sections.relevance_reason: APRA requires security assessments for third parties handling personal data

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must actively maintain its information security
sections.title: Maintain information security capability through threat monitoring
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must continuously update their information security capabilities to address evolving vulnerabilities and threats. This includes adapting to changes in information assets or business environments. The requirement emphasizes active maintenance rather than static security measures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates ongoing security updates, critical for DTIA as it requires continuous risk assessment and adaptation to changing threats when handling cross-border data.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must actively maintain information security capability with respect to changes in vulnerabilities and threats
This includes threats resulting from changes to information assets or business environment

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must actively maintain its information security capability with respect to changes in vulnerabilities and threats, including those resulting from changes to information assets or its business environment.
sections.relevance_reason: APRA security requirements for personal data

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must maintain an information security policy
sections.title: Maintain information security policy framework
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must maintain an information security policy framework that matches their exposure to vulnerabilities and threats. This framework must be tailored to the specific risks the entity faces, ensuring appropriate security measures are in place.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires a risk-based security policy framework, directly relevant to DTIA as it mandates alignment between security policies and potential data transfer risks.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must maintain an information security policy framework
Framework must be commensurate with exposures to vulnerabilities and threats

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must maintain an information security policy framework commensurate with its exposures to vulnerabilities and threats.
sections.relevance_reason: Core security policy for data protection

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity's information security policy framework must provide
sections.title: Define information security responsibilities for all parties
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must ensure their information security policy framework specifies responsibilities for all parties obligated to maintain information security. This includes staff, contractors, related parties, third parties, and customers, ensuring comprehensive accountability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies responsibility allocation for third parties, critical for DTIA as it defines who is accountable for data security when transferring information across borders.

requirements.text: Information security policy framework must provide direction on responsibilities of all parties with obligation to maintain information security

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity's information security policy framework must provide direction on the responsibilities of all parties who have an obligation to maintain information security.
sections.relevance_reason: Core data security responsibilities for regulated entities

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must classify its information assets, including those
sections.title: Classify information assets by criticality and sensitivity
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must classify their information assets based on criticality and sensitivity, reflecting the potential financial or non-financial impact of security incidents. This classification includes assets managed by related parties and third parties, ensuring appropriate security measures are applied.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires data classification for DTIA, as it mandates assessing the impact of security incidents on financial and customer interests when transferring data internationally.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must classify information assets by criticality and sensitivity
Classification must reflect potential financial or non-financial impact of security incidents on entity or customers

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must classify its information assets, including those managed by related parties and third parties, by criticality and sensitivity. This classification must reflect the degree to which an information security incident affecting an information asset has the potential to affect, financially or non-financially, the entity or the interests of depositors, policyholders, beneficiaries or other customers.
sections.relevance_reason: APRA classification for security incidents impacts data handling

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must have information security controls to protect its
sections.title: Implement security controls commensurate with risk
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must implement information security controls that are timely and proportionate to vulnerabilities, threats, asset criticality, sensitivity, life-cycle stage, and potential incident consequences. These controls must protect information assets managed by related parties and third parties.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires risk-based security controls for DTIA, ensuring that data transfers are protected by measures proportional to the potential impact on financial stakeholders.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must have information security controls to protect information assets
Controls must be implemented in a timely manner
Controls must be commensurate with vulnerabilities and threats to information assets
Controls must be commensurate with criticality and sensitivity of information assets
Controls must be commensurate with stage of information assets within their life-cycle
Controls must be commensurate with potential consequences of information security incidents

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must have information security controls to protect its information assets, including those managed by related parties and third parties, that are implemented in a timely manner and that are commensurate with: (a) vulnerabilities and threats to the information assets; (b) the criticality and sensitivity of the information assets; (c) the stage at which the information assets are within their life-cycle; and (d) the potential consequences of an information security incident.
sections.relevance_reason: Core security controls for data protection

sections.heading: Where an APRA-regulated entity's information assets are managed by a related
sections.title: Information security controls for related parties
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must evaluate the design of information security controls managed by related parties or third parties that protect their information assets. This applies to all information assets handled by such parties, including those under outsourcing agreements. The evaluation ensures adequate protection mechanisms are in place for sensitive data.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires assessment of third-party security controls before data transfers involving related entities, critical for DTIA to verify cross-border data protection adequacy.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must evaluate design of information security controls protecting its information assets managed by related parties or third parties

sections.source_quote: Where an APRA-regulated entity's information assets are managed by a related party or third party, the APRA-regulated entity must evaluate the design of that party's information security controls that protects the information assets of the APRA-regulated entity.
sections.relevance_reason: APRA-regulated entities must evaluate security controls for third parties handling personal data

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must have robust mechanisms in place to detect and
sections.title: Timely incident detection and response mechanisms
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must establish robust mechanisms to detect and respond to information security incidents promptly. This ensures rapid containment and mitigation of potential breaches, minimizing financial and operational impacts on the entity.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates real-time incident response capabilities, crucial for DTIA to assess timely breach containment during cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must have robust mechanisms to detect and respond to information security incidents in a timely manner

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must have robust mechanisms in place to detect and respond to information security incidents in a timely manner.
sections.relevance_reason: APRA requires timely incident detection/response for breaches

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must maintain plans to respond to information security
sections.title: Information security incident response plans
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must maintain detailed response plans for information security incidents that could plausibly occur. These plans must address potential threats and ensure readiness to mitigate risks effectively.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires proactive planning for potential incidents, essential for DTIA to evaluate preparedness in cross-border data environments.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must maintain plans to respond to information security incidents that the entity considers could plausibly occur

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must maintain plans to respond to information security incidents that the entity considers could plausibly occur (information security response plans).
sections.relevance_reason: core breach notification requirement

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity's information security response plans must include the
sections.title: Incident response plan components
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities' incident response plans must include mechanisms for managing incident stages from detection to post-incident review and for escalating incidents to appropriate oversight bodies. This ensures comprehensive incident handling and accountability.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies detailed incident management requirements, critical for DTIA to assess thoroughness in cross-border data incident handling.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity's information security response plans must include mechanisms for managing all relevant stages of an incident from detection to post-incident review

APRA-regulated entity's information security response plans must include mechanisms for escalation and reporting of information security incidents to the Board, other governing bodies and individuals responsible for information security incident management and oversight

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity's information security response plans must include the mechanisms in place for: (a) managing all relevant stages of an incident, from detection to post-incident review; and (b) escalation and reporting of information security incidents to the Board, other governing bodies and individuals responsible for information security incident management and oversight, as appropriate.
sections.relevance_reason: Incident response for breaches and APRA oversight

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must annually review and test its information security
sections.title: Annual review and testing of response plans
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must annually review and test their information security response plans to ensure they remain effective and fit for purpose. This ongoing validation maintains the relevance and reliability of incident response capabilities.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates annual testing of incident response plans, vital for DTIA to confirm ongoing effectiveness in cross-border data scenarios.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must annually review and test its information security response plans to ensure they remain effective and fit-for-purpose

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must annually review and test its information security response plans to ensure they remain effective and fit-for-purpose.
sections.relevance_reason: Annual security response plan testing ensures breach readiness

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must test the effectiveness of its information security
sections.title: Systematic testing of security controls
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must conduct systematic testing of information security controls with frequency and scope commensurate to threat changes, asset criticality, incident consequences, policy enforcement risks, and asset changes. This ensures controls remain effective against evolving threats.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires tailored testing of security controls, critical for DTIA to assess adequacy of cross-border data protection mechanisms.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must test effectiveness of information security controls through systematic testing program
Nature and frequency of systematic testing must be commensurate with rate of change in vulnerabilities and threats
Nature and frequency of systematic testing must be commensurate with criticality and sensitivity of information asset
Nature and frequency of systematic testing must be commensurate with consequences of information security incident
Nature and frequency of systematic testing must be commensurate with risks associated with exposure to environments where APRA-regulated entity cannot enforce information security policies
Nature and frequency of systematic testing must be commensurate with materiality and frequency of change to information assets

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must test the effectiveness of its information security controls through a systematic testing program. The nature and frequency of the systematic testing must be commensurate with: (a) the rate at which the vulnerabilities and threats change; (b) the criticality and sensitivity of the information asset; (c) the consequences of an information security incident; (d) the risks associated with exposure to environments where the APRA-regulated entity is unable to enforce its information security policies; and (e) the materiality and frequency of change to information assets.
sections.relevance_reason: Systematic testing of security controls ensures data protection and security compliance

sections.heading: Where an APRA-regulated entity's information assets are managed by a related
sections.title: Information security testing adequacy for related parties
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must assess whether the frequency and nature of information security control testing by related parties or third parties aligns with specified prudential standards. This assessment is required when the entity relies on those parties' testing for security controls. The assessment ensures controls meet minimum standards for information assets managed externally.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires entities to verify that external parties' security testing meets baseline standards before relying on their controls, impacting cross-border data transfers involving third parties.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must assess whether the nature and frequency of testing of controls in respect of information assets is commensurate with paragraphs 27(a) to 27(e) of this Prudential Standard

sections.source_quote: Where an APRA-regulated entity's information assets are managed by a related party or a third party, and the APRA-regulated entity is reliant on that party's information security control testing, the APRA-regulated entity must assess whether the nature and frequency of testing of controls in respect of those information assets is commensurate with paragraphs 27(a) to 27(e) of this Prudential Standard.
sections.relevance_reason: APRA security testing for related parties

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must escalate and report to the Board or senior
sections.title: Escalation of critical security control deficiencies
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must escalate and report to their Board or senior management any information security control deficiencies that cannot be fixed promptly. This requirement applies to deficiencies identified through testing that pose significant risks to the entity or its customers. The escalation ensures timely resolution of critical security issues.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates reporting of unremediable security control gaps to leadership, critical for assessing risks in data transfers involving third parties.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must escalate and report to the Board or senior management any testing results that identify information security control deficiencies that cannot be remediated in a timely manner

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must escalate and report to the Board or senior management any testing results that identify information security control deficiencies that cannot be remediated in a timely manner.
sections.relevance_reason: Critical security control deficiencies impact data protection and breach response

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must review the sufficiency of the testing program at
sections.title: Annual review of information security testing program
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must review the sufficiency of their information security testing program at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to information assets or the business environment. This review ensures the testing program remains aligned with evolving risks and operational needs, particularly for data transfers involving third parties.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires periodic review of security testing adequacy, critical for maintaining robust data transfer safeguards with external parties.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must review the sufficiency of the testing program at least annually or when there is a material change to information assets or the business environment

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must review the sufficiency of the testing program at least annually or when there is a material change to information assets or the business environment.
sections.relevance_reason: Annual security testing review for data transfers

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity's internal audit activities must include a review of the
sections.title: Internal audit review of third-party security controls
sections.summary: Internal audit activities must include reviewing the design and operating effectiveness of information security controls maintained by related parties and third parties. This ensures that external parties' security controls provide adequate assurance before being relied upon by the entity, particularly for critical information assets.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires internal audits to validate third-party security controls, essential for assessing data transfer risks with external entities.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity's internal audit activities must include a review of the design and operating effectiveness of information security controls, including those maintained by related parties and third parties (information security control assurance)

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity's internal audit activities must include a review of the design and operating effectiveness of information security controls, including those maintained by related parties and third parties (information security control assurance).
sections.relevance_reason: Internal audit of third-party security controls

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must ensure that the information security control
sections.title: Skilled personnel for information security assurance
sections.summary: APRA-regulated entities must ensure that personnel providing information security control assurance are appropriately skilled. This requirement ensures that external parties' security assessments are conducted by qualified individuals, reducing risks associated with inadequate security evaluations.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates skilled personnel for third-party security assurance, critical for validating data transfer security.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity must ensure that the information security control assurance is provided by personnel appropriately skilled in providing such assurance

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must ensure that the information security control assurance is provided by personnel appropriately skilled in providing such assurance.
sections.relevance_reason: APRA security assurance for personnel

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity's internal audit function must assess the information
sections.title: Internal audit assessment of third-party security incidents
sections.summary: Internal audit must assess information security control assurance provided by related parties or third parties when there is a potential for significant financial or non-financial impact from incidents affecting information assets, or when internal audit intends to rely on those parties' assurance. This ensures critical risks are addressed before external parties are used for data processing.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires internal audits to evaluate third-party security assurance in high-risk scenarios, directly impacting data transfer security.

requirements.text: APRA-regulated entity's internal audit function must assess the information security control assurance provided by a related party or third party where: (a) an information security incident affecting the information assets has the potential to materially affect, financially or non-financially, the entity or the interests of depositors, policyholders, beneficiaries or other customers; and (b) internal audit intends to rely on the information security control assurance provided by the related party or third party

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity's internal audit function must assess the information security control assurance provided by a related party or third party where: (a) an information security incident affecting the information assets has the potential to materially affect, financially or non-financially, the entity or the interests of depositors, policyholders, beneficiaries or other customers; and (b) internal audit intends to rely on the information security control assurance provided by the related party or third party.
sections.relevance_reason: Internal audit for third-party security incidents

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must notify APRA as soon as possible and, in any
sections.title: Notification of material information security incidents to APRA
sections.summary: An APRA-regulated entity must notify APRA within 72 hours of becoming aware of an information security incident that materially affected or had the potential to materially affect financially or non-financially the entity or its customers. The incident must involve depositors, policyholders, beneficiaries, or other customers. The notification must be made as soon as possible, with a maximum deadline of 72 hours.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires timely reporting of material information security incidents that could impact financial or non-financial interests, directly affecting DTIA timelines for incident response and cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: An APRA-regulated entity must notify APRA as soon as possible
An APRA-regulated entity must notify APRA no later than 72 hours after becoming aware of an information security incident

The information security incident must materially affect or have the potential to materially affect financially or non-financially the entity or the interests of depositors, policyholders, beneficiaries or other customers

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must notify APRA as soon as possible and, in any case, no later than 72 hours, after becoming aware of an information security incident that: (a) materially affected, or had the potential to materially affect, financially or non-financially, the entity or the interests of depositors, policyholders, beneficiaries or other customers; or (b) has been notified to other regulators, either in Australia or other jurisdictions.
sections.relevance_reason: Breach notification for financial data

sections.heading: An APRA-regulated entity must notify APRA as soon as possible and, in any
sections.title: Notification of material information security control weaknesses to APRA
sections.summary: An APRA-regulated entity must notify APRA within 10 business days of becoming aware of a material information security control weakness that the entity expects it will not be able to remediate in a timely manner. This applies to weaknesses that could impact the entity's operations or customer interests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates reporting of unresolved information security control weaknesses, which impacts DTIA assessments for third-party risk and remediation timelines in cross-border data environments.

requirements.text: An APRA-regulated entity must notify APRA as soon as possible
An APRA-regulated entity must notify APRA no later than 10 business days after becoming aware of a material information security control weakness
The information security control weakness must be one the entity expects it will not be able to remediate in a timely manner

sections.source_quote: An APRA-regulated entity must notify APRA as soon as possible and, in any case, no later than 10 business days, after it becomes aware of a material information security control weakness which the entity expects it will not be able to remediate in a timely manner.
sections.relevance_reason: core breach notification for APRA-regulated entities
